examples.title: Modus Ponens Implementation
examples.description: Basic inference rule for forward chaining
examples.code:
FUNCTION modus_ponens(kb, P, P_implies_Q):
 IF kb.contains(P) AND kb.contains(P_implies_Q) THEN
 // Extract Q from P→Q
 consequent = extract_consequent(P_implies_Q)
 kb.add(consequent)
 RETURN consequent
 RETURN NULL

FUNCTION forward_chaining(kb, rules, goal):
 new_inferred = TRUE
 WHILE new_inferred:
 new_inferred = FALSE
 FOR EACH rule IN rules:
 // Check if all antecedents are satisfied
 IF all_antecedents_true(kb, rule.antecedents):
 consequent = rule.consequent
 IF NOT kb.contains(consequent):
 kb.add(consequent)
 new_inferred = TRUE
 IF consequent == goal:
 RETURN SUCCESS
 RETURN FAILURE
examples.explanation: Forward chaining applies Modus Ponens repeatedly. When all antecedents of a rule are true, add the consequent to knowledge base. Continues until goal reached or no new facts.

examples.title: Unification Algorithm
examples.description: Finding substitutions to make two FOL expressions identical
examples.code:
FUNCTION unify(x, y, theta):
 IF theta = FAIL THEN RETURN FAIL
 IF x == y THEN RETURN theta
 IF variable(x) THEN RETURN unify_var(x, y, theta)
 IF variable(y) THEN RETURN unify_var(y, x, theta)
 IF compound(x) OR compound(y) THEN:
 IF compound(x) OR compound(y) THEN:
 RETURN FAIL
 RETURN unify(x.args, y.args, unify(x.op, y.op, theta))
 RETURN FAIL

FUNCTION unify_var(var, term, theta):
 IF theta.contains(var) THEN RETURN unify(theta[var], term, theta)
 IF occurs_check(var, term) THEN RETURN FAIL
 RETURN extend(theta, var, term)

FUNCTION occurs_check(var, term):
 IF var == term THEN RETURN TRUE
 IF variable(term) THEN RETURN FALSE
 IF compound(term) THEN:
 FOR EACH arg IN term.args:
 IF occurs_check(var, arg) THEN RETURN TRUE
 RETURN FALSE
examples.explanation: Unification finds the most general substitution that makes two expressions identical. Uses occurs_check to prevent infinite structures. Essential for first-order inference.

examples.title: Resolution Inference Rule
examples.code:
FUNCTION resolve(clause1, clause2):
 resolvents = []
 FOR EACH literal1 IN clause1.literals:
 FOR EACH literal2 IN clause2.literals:
 // Check for complementary literals
 IF complementary(literal1, literal2):
 // Resolve on these literals
 new_literals = remove(literal1, clause1) ∪ 
 remove(literal2, clause2)
 // Apply substitution if needed
 subst = unify(literal1, neg(literal2), {})
 new_clause = apply_substitution(new_literals, subst)
 resolvents.add(new_clause)
 RETURN resolvents

FUNCTION complementary(lit1, lit2):
 RETURN (lit1.predicate == lit2.predicate AND 
 lit1.sign != lit2.sign)

FUNCTION resolution_proof(kb, goal):
 clauses = kb.clauses ∪ {neg(goal)}
 new = TRUE
 WHILE new:
 new = FALSE
 pairs = generate_pairs(clauses)
 FOR EACH pair IN pairs:
 resolvents = resolve(pair.cl1, pair.cl2)
 FOR EACH res IN resolvents:
 IF res.empty() THEN RETURN PROOF_FOUND
 IF NOT clause_exists(clauses, res):
 clauses.add(res)
 new = TRUE
 RETURN NO_PROOF
examples.explanation: Resolution is complete for first-order logic. It resolves complementary literals from two clauses to produce a new clause. Empty clause (contradiction) proves the goal.
